narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.60—above the typical 3.21 average—your law school application stood out in some ways, but the admissions model focused on predicting your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model assessed your overall profile and estimated an 84.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which directly led to the rejection of your application since the school prioritizes candidates likely to succeed on that critical test. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, placing you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year, well below the average decile of about 5.5. This low performance in your later undergraduate years signaled to the model a potential struggle with the sustained rigor needed for bar success. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, also a 1—again far below the typical 5.7 average—which reinforced concerns about your early academic trajectory not building the strong foundation most successful bar takers have. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA actually pulled the prediction somewhat toward passing, as it exceeded the average and suggested capability in a broader college context. Other details like your middle family income bracket, LSAT score of 27 (below the 36.7 average), and full-time student status—common among 93% of applicants—played supporting roles but weren't the top drivers. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your profile shows weaker high school decile rankings that outweigh your GPA strength, tipping the model's decision against admission. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background; it's the model highlighting patterns from past students where low deciles like yours correlated strongly with bar failures. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that address your academic growth. Reapplying after addressing these key areas could shift your predicted bar success odds significantly. Many students in similar situations have improved their profiles and succeeded—your above-average GPA is a great starting point for that effort.
